practical_and_experimental_content: Demonstrate an appreciation of safety in the laboratory and apply general laboratory safety rules.
Demonstrate the changes of states of matter (melting, freezing, boiling, condensation, sublimation).
Demonstrate the movement of particles (diffusion) in fluids.
Demonstrate how different quantities are measured (time, temperature, mass, and volume).
Identify and safely use measuring apparatus (stopwatches, thermometers, balances, burettes, pipettes, volumetric flasks, measuring cylinders, and gas syringes).
Demonstrate determination of the purity of a substance using melting and boiling points.
Demonstrate different methods of separating mixtures (decantation, filtration, crystallisation, distillation, magnetism, chromatography, evaporation, centrifugation).
Interpret simple paper chromatograms.

subtopics: 11.5.1 Characteristic properties of acids and bases
11.5.4 Identification of ions and gases (Qualitative analysis)
practical_and_experimental_content: Investigate the acidity and alkalinity of substances in everyday life.
Demonstrate the preparation of an insoluble salt using precipitation and separation by filtration.
Demonstrate the preparation of soluble salts by reaction of acids with bases, metals, and carbonates, followed by crystallisation and filtration.
Demonstrate the preparation of ammonium, potassium, and sodium salts.
Perform qualitative analysis to identify unknown cations, anions, and gases.
Demonstrate acid-base titrations to solve problems involving concentration and the dilution law.
Determine the rate of chemical reactions experimentally.
Plan and conduct investigations to show that respiration and photosynthesis involve energy changes (exothermic and endothermic reactions).
Investigate the physical and chemical properties of transition elements experimentally.
Plan and conduct an experiment to investigate electrical conductors and non-conductors.
Experiment with electrolytic cells to classify electrolytes and non-electrolytes.
Formulate hypotheses and observe products at the electrodes during the electrolysis of molten binary ionic compounds and aqueous ionic solutions.
Demonstrate an order of reactivity from a set of experimental results (e.g., reduction of metallic oxides by other metals).
Investigate and describe the effects of heat on hydroxides, carbonates, nitrates of metals and ammonium compounds.
Demonstrate the laboratory methods for the preparation, collection, and testing of hydrogen chloride gas.
Demonstrate the method for the preparation of hydrochloric acid by dissolving gas in water.
Demonstrate the laboratory preparation, collection, and test for sulphur dioxide.
Investigate physical and chemical properties of non-metals and their corresponding gases experimentally.
Conduct chemical tests for unsaturated hydrocarbons (alkenes) using bromine solution and observing colour changes.
Use models to demonstrate the structures of isomers and the concept of catenation.
